guide.machine: KUKA robot cell with a hydraulic B-axis/clamp system
guide.summary: This video works through a hydraulic motion problem on a KUKA-controlled machine, where alarms appear, the B-axis will not move normally, and the team suspects trapped air or incorrect pressure settings. The main path is to compare machine settings against the book, restore a changed value, clear alarms, confirm motion, and then prepare to bleed the hydraulic system while checking the correct pressure references.
guide.overview: You’re looking at a robot cell where the KUKA teach pendant is used to reset alarms and command motion, while a separate hydraulic system handles part of the machine’s axis or clamp behavior. What’s happening here is that motion depends on both controls and hydraulics: the control side has to be reset correctly, and the hydraulic side has to hold the right pressure so the axis can move and hold safely. The team keeps comparing values from the book, the machine door, and the live settings because not every printed number appears to match what this machine actually needs. Before they open anything, they prove the machine can move again, then they shift into hydraulic diagnosis, looking for vent ports and a manifold so they can bleed trapped air out of the system.

machineIntro.title: KUKA teach pendant and control resets
machineIntro.detail: This is the handheld robot controller you use to navigate the control screen, reset alarms, and command motion. In the video, they distinguish between a PLC alarm reset and a non-PLC alarm reset, which matters because using the right reset is part of getting the machine responsive again.

machineIntro.title: Hydraulic system for the B-axis/clamp
machineIntro.detail: This hydraulic system uses pressure to let the machine move and hold position. You’ll notice they focus on the B-axis first because it can rotate, and they treat that differently from the Z-axis, which could drop if pressure is lost.

machineIntro.title: Set points and pressure references
machineIntro.detail: Set points are the target values the machine uses to decide when pressure is high enough or low enough. In this video, the team compares values in the book, values on the machine door, and live machine settings because they suspect some settings were changed and because the printed diagram may be generic rather than exact for this machine.

machineIntro.title: Manifold and vent ports
machineIntro.detail: The manifold is the hydraulic block where valves and passages come together, and the vent ports are the places they expect to bleed air from the system. Once the cover comes off, that’s the area they’re trying to locate from the diagram.

troubleshooting.title: Book value and machine setting do not match
troubleshooting.symptom: You notice a setting is at 1,000 on the machine, but the book says 1,300.
troubleshooting.story:
Start here, because mismatched settings can send you chasing a hydraulic problem that’s partly just a parameter problem. Right at the beginning, they spot that one value is 1,000 even though the book says 1,300, and they decide to put that one back. That tells you something important: before you tear into hardware, compare what the machine is actually set to against the references you trust.

As the video goes on, you’ll notice they become more cautious about trusting every printed number equally. They later decide the value on the machine door is probably more correct than the generic drawing for this machine. So the lesson is not just 'compare numbers'—it’s 'compare numbers and decide which reference belongs to this exact machine.'

fix.text: Identify the setting that is at 1,000 and compare it to the reference that says it should be 1,300.
fix.expected: You confirm there is a mismatch between the live setting and the reference value.
fix.visual: the black rotary setting knob near the upper-right of the panel

fix.text: Set that value back to 1,300.
fix.expected: The setting now matches the referenced value from the book.
fix.visual: the round digital pressure gauge near the upper left of the machine frame

fix.text: Continue checking whether the machine door values make more sense than the generic drawing for this machine.
fix.expected: You have a more reliable reference for later pressure checks.
fix.visual: the round viewing port on the left door panel

troubleshooting.verify: After restoring the value, continue with reset and motion testing. If alarms clear and motion improves, the setting change was at least part of the problem.
troubleshooting.ifNotResolved: Move on to alarm reset and hydraulic checks. The video suggests the machine may still need bleeding even after settings are corrected.

troubleshooting.title: Right side light goes off and alarms need reset
troubleshooting.symptom: The right side light goes off, and the machine needs to be reset before motion returns.
troubleshooting.story:
When a status light changes, that’s your first clue that the control side has dropped out or changed state. In the video, the right side light goes off, and they immediately go back to control to try a reset. That’s a good habit: don’t assume a hydraulic fault until you’ve made the control system happy again.

What’s happening here is that different alarms may clear in different ways. They mention one reset for the PLC alarm and another for the non-PLC alarm. Then a large group of alarms appears and disappears during testing, which they treat as expected test alarms rather than a new failure. So your job is to reset, watch what comes back, and separate real faults from test noise.

fix.text: Go back to the control screen when the right side light goes off.
fix.expected: You are ready to perform the proper reset from the control interface.
fix.visual: the round black sensor/display on the lower-left of the machine

fix.text: Use the PLC alarm reset if the fault is a PLC alarm.
fix.expected: PLC-related alarms should clear if that was the active fault.
fix.visual: the PLC reset button on the lower-right control panel beneath the screen

fix.text: Use the non-PLC reset for non-PLC alarms.
fix.expected: Non-PLC alarms should clear if that was the active fault.
fix.visual: the red emergency-stop button below the monitor

fix.text: Watch the alarm list during testing and note whether alarms come up and then go away.
fix.expected: Test alarms may appear briefly and then clear.
fix.visual: the alarm list on the KRC Expert 2 control screen

troubleshooting.verify: You know this part is fixed when the alarms clear and the machine will let you try moving the B-axis again.
troubleshooting.ifNotResolved: Recheck whether you used the correct reset path, then continue into pressure and bleed diagnosis if the machine still will not move correctly.

troubleshooting.title: B-axis will not move normally until reset and pressure issues are addressed
troubleshooting.symptom: After reset, the B-axis moves again, but not through full expected travel and the team still suspects a hydraulic issue.
troubleshooting.story:
Once the alarms are out of the way, the next question is simple: can the axis move? In the video, after testing, they say you should be able to move the B-axis, and then they confirm the machine is moving. That’s real progress, but not the end of the story, because they still don’t like how the set points had to be changed to get there.

You’ll notice they test range instead of stopping at 'it moved once.' That’s the right instinct. The axis can go farther, but not complete; they mention about 170 degrees instead of a complete flip. That tells you the system is alive but may still be weak, air-bound, or set incorrectly. In other words, partial motion is a clue, not a victory lap.

fix.text: After alarms are cleared, try moving the B-axis from the control.
fix.expected: The machine should begin moving again if control reset was successful.
fix.visual: the KRC control screen on the teach pendant monitor

fix.text: Use the teach pendant controls and rotary knob as needed to command motion.
fix.expected: You should see some motion progress.
fix.visual: the dual rotary override knobs on the right side of the teach pendant panel

fix.text: Test how far the axis can travel instead of checking only whether it moves at all.
fix.expected: You learn whether the axis has full range or only partial range.
fix.visual: the K2 robot controller cabinet on the right side of the frame

fix.text: Stop short if needed so the whole weight does not slide off as soon as you try to take it off.
fix.expected: The setup remains controlled and supported during testing.
fix.visual: the black-and-red emergency stop button below the monitor on the control panel

troubleshooting.verify: This is improved when the B-axis moves and can go farther than before. Full confidence comes only after pressure checks and bleeding if needed.
troubleshooting.ifNotResolved: Check the pressure relationship and bleed the hydraulic system, because the team believes hydraulics are still part of the problem.

troubleshooting.title: Hydraulic system likely has air and needs bleeding
troubleshooting.symptom: The machine moves some, but the team believes the hydraulics still are not right and decides the system needs to be bled.
troubleshooting.story:
This is the heart of the repair. Bleeding means letting trapped air out of a hydraulic system so the fluid can do its job solidly. Air compresses; hydraulic oil is meant to act much more firmly. When they say 'we need to bleed it,' what they’re really saying is that the axis behavior still feels wrong even after control resets and set point checks.

Before opening anything, they reason through the pressure logic and decide the pressure should be between SP1 and RP1. Then they prepare the area, shut off coolant, and shut off hydraulics before cracking anything open. That sequence matters because once you loosen a hydraulic connection, fluid can spray and pressure loss can change how the axis behaves.

You’ll also notice the caution about axis type. For the B-axis, losing pressure means it may rotate freely, which they consider manageable. For the Z-axis, losing pressure could let the whole axis drop. That’s the kind of machine judgment you want to build: same hydraulic action, very different mechanical risk depending on which axis you’re touching.

fix.text: Confirm the pressure you are watching is the one that has to be between SP1 and RP1.
fix.expected: You know which pressure relationship you are diagnosing before bleeding.
fix.visual: the pressure reading on the KRC control screen in the center of the cabinet

fix.text: Decide to bleed the system if motion is still not right and you suspect trapped air.
fix.expected: You have a clear next diagnostic step.

fix.text: Turn off coolant if it is in the way.
fix.expected: The work area is easier to access and observe.
fix.visual: the black corrugated coolant hose on the right side

fix.text: Turn off the hydraulics before opening the hydraulic connection.
fix.expected: The system is no longer actively pressurizing the line you are about to open.

fix.text: Crack the hydraulic open just a little bit to bleed it.
fix.expected: Fluid may spray and trapped air may begin to vent.
fix.visual: the small bleed screw/valve on the top-right side of the hydraulic tank

fix.text: Keep the B-axis risk in mind if pressure is lost, because it may rotate freely.
fix.expected: You stay prepared for free rotation on the B-axis.
fix.visual: the B-axis locking handle on top of the gray rotary housing

fix.text: Use extra caution and avoid the same casual approach on the Z-axis, because losing pressure there can let the whole axis drop down.
fix.expected: You prevent a dangerous axis drop.
fix.visual: the large white cylindrical Z-axis housing in the center of the machine

troubleshooting.verify: After bleeding, the machine should move more normally and pressure behavior should make sense relative to the set points being monitored.
troubleshooting.ifNotResolved: Locate the correct vent ports and manifold from the diagram, then continue bleeding from the proper point. Also recheck whether the machine door values are the correct pressure reference for this machine.

troubleshooting.title: Need to find the correct bleed point, manifold, and vent ports
troubleshooting.symptom: You know the system likely needs bleeding, but you cannot yet see the correct vent ports or manifold access point.
troubleshooting.story:
A lot of hydraulic troubleshooting is really access and identification. In the video, once they commit to bleeding, they stop guessing and go hunting for the diagram. That’s smart. Rather than opening random fittings, they want the manifold cover off and the vent ports identified from the picture.

You’ll notice some confusion because one diagram turns out to be electrical, not the hydraulic overview they expected. That’s a common field problem: you have documentation, but not the exact page you need. They keep working the chain—email, screenshot, chat, page reference—until they get closer to the right view. The practical lesson is to verify the diagram matches the system in front of you before you trust it.

Once the big cover comes off, they expect to see two ventilation ports for the hydraulic system. That becomes the visual target. If you’re standing there with hoses and fittings in front of you and wondering what to open, stop and identify those ports first.

fix.text: Open the cover to gain access to the hydraulic area.
fix.expected: The manifold area becomes visible or closer to visible.
fix.visual: the black latch on top of the round gray cover

fix.text: Get the hydraulic diagram and send or share it with the person helping diagnose.
fix.expected: You have a reference for the manifold and vent locations.

fix.text: Take off the big cover if that is the cover blocking access.
fix.expected: You can inspect the area under the large cover.
fix.visual: the large cover on the upper-right side of the machine diagram on the laptop screen

fix.text: Look for the manifold under the cover.
fix.expected: You identify the hydraulic block area where the vent ports should be.
fix.visual: the large round gray cover in the center of the machine

fix.text: Locate the two ventilation ports for the hydraulic system.
fix.expected: You find the bleed targets shown in the picture.
fix.visual: the two small round ports on the upper-left side of the white hydraulic tank

fix.text: Use the highlighted picture to confirm you are looking at the correct ports.
fix.expected: The physical machine matches the diagram more clearly.
fix.visual: blue circular port panel with a red connector near the upper-right side of the machine

troubleshooting.verify: You know this step is complete when you can point to the manifold and the two vent ports with confidence.
troubleshooting.ifNotResolved: Keep tracing documentation. The team specifically looks for a fuller hydraulic connection diagram rather than an exploded view or electrical diagram.

troubleshooting.title: Pressure references in the drawing do not seem to match the machine
troubleshooting.symptom: The drawing shows low values like 5 bar, 80 PSI, and 40 PSI, while the machine appears to use much higher values around 1000, 1300, 1500, and 1520.
troubleshooting.story:
This is where you have to think like a technician instead of just reading numbers. They find a page showing BP2, SP1 at 5 bar / 80 PSI, RP1 at 40 PSI, and also 1520 and 1300. Then they compare that to what the machine is actually showing and realize some values are nowhere close. That mismatch is the clue that the page may be generic or only partly applicable.

The explanation given in the video is that factory testing may tune settings and parameters, and each machine can need a little more or a little less. That’s why they stop treating the drawing as absolute truth and start trusting the value on the door more. The teaching point here is simple: if a printed pressure value makes no physical sense for the way the machine is behaving, don’t force the machine to match the paper without checking whether the paper is generic.

They also note that the pressure has to be high enough and that the low and high sides are close together by design on these machines. So when you diagnose this kind of issue, compare all references, ask which one belongs to this exact machine, and make sure the pressure logic makes sense before changing anything else.

fix.text: Read the pressure labels shown on the diagram, including BP2, SP1, RP1, and the values 1520 and 1300.
fix.expected: You understand what the document is claiming.
fix.visual: the pressure diagram sheet on the paper in the center of the frame

fix.text: Compare those values to the live machine values and note where they differ significantly.
fix.expected: You identify which numbers do not match the machine.
fix.visual: the printed wiring schematic sheet being pointed at on the right side

fix.text: Treat the drawing as possibly generic if the values do not make sense for this machine.
fix.expected: You avoid forcing the machine to a value that may not belong to it.
fix.visual: the paper schematic drawing being held up on the machine console

fix.text: Use the values on the machine door as the more likely correct reference if they fit the machine behavior better.
fix.expected: You have a practical reference point for pressure diagnosis.
fix.visual: the circular black handwheel on the left side of the machine door

fix.text: Keep in mind that the machine is designed with very exact tolerances and close clamp and unclamp values.
fix.expected: You understand why the pressure window may be tight.
fix.visual: the black circular clamp mechanism inside the hexagonal opening on the upper-right machine panel

troubleshooting.verify: You know this is sorted out when your chosen reference values make sense with the machine’s actual operation and the team agrees which source to trust.
troubleshooting.ifNotResolved: Get the fuller hydraulic connection diagram from the email chain or manufacturer contact, because the team specifically wanted more than the exploded or electrical pages.

troubleshooting.title: Bleed point hardware is visible, but the exact release method is unclear
troubleshooting.symptom: After removing the cover and aiming at the fitting area, you can see hoses and connectors, but you are unsure whether to pressurize, use a switch, loosen a bolt, or push into a port.
troubleshooting.story:
This is the moment where a lot of damage can happen if you rush. In the video, once they finally have the cover off and the hose-and-fitting cluster in view, they still pause because they are not sure exactly how this bleed point works. That hesitation is healthy. If you don’t know whether a fitting is a vent, a quick-connect, or a pressurized plug, guessing can turn into a spray or a dropped axis.

They compare it loosely to bleeding brakes, but immediately admit that this is not the same setup. Then they observe that it looks like something pushes into the port, though they do not have that piece. That tells you the diagnosis has reached the hardware, but the procedure is still incomplete. The right move at that point is to stop, keep the picture up, and positively identify the bleed interface before forcing anything.

fix.text: Aim the view or tool at the suspected bleed area so the helper can see what you are seeing.
fix.expected: The fitting area is visible for comparison to the diagram.
fix.visual: the black circular cable ring on top of the white robot base

fix.text: Do not guess whether to pressurize, use a switch, or loosen a bolt until the fitting type is identified.
fix.expected: You avoid opening the wrong point or releasing pressure unsafely.

fix.text: Confirm that the cover has been removed and the correct hose-and-connector cluster is exposed.
fix.expected: You are working at the intended access point.

fix.text: Compare the visible port to the picture and determine whether it is a push-in style connection.
fix.expected: You narrow down how the bleed point is meant to be used.

fix.text: Share a live picture with the remote helper if needed so they can confirm the hardware.
fix.expected: You get a second set of eyes before acting.

troubleshooting.verify: This step is complete when you can identify the exact bleed interface and know how it is meant to be opened or connected.
troubleshooting.ifNotResolved: Pause the repair and get the correct hydraulic procedure or clearer diagram, because the video does not confirm the final bleed connection method.

safety.title: Hydraulic spray when cracking a line
safety.detail: Opening a hydraulic connection can spray hard because pressure may still be in the system. The team explicitly warns that it will spray when cracked open.
steps: Turn the hydraulics off before opening the connection.
Crack the fitting open only a little bit.
Keep people out of the spray path.

safety.title: B-axis may rotate freely if pressure is lost
safety.detail: If pressure is lost on the B-axis, it may rotate freely. In the video they consider that manageable, but it still means the axis can move unexpectedly.
steps: Support or control the axis before bleeding.
Do not place yourself where free rotation could hit you.
Test movement carefully after pressure changes.

safety.title: Z-axis can drop if pressure is lost
safety.detail: The Z-axis is the more dangerous case mentioned in the video. If you lose pressure there, the whole axis can drop down.
steps: Be really careful when working on the Z-axis.
Do not bleed or open hydraulic points on the Z-axis casually.
Prevent the axis from dropping before releasing pressure.

safety.title: Weight shifting during range testing
safety.detail: During motion testing, they stop with the axis positioned so the whole weight does not slide off when they try to take it off. That means load position matters while diagnosing.
steps: Do not run the axis farther than needed for diagnosis.
Keep the load in a position where it will not slide off.
Think about gravity before removing or loosening anything.

glossary.definition: An alarm tied to the programmable logic controller, the control system that runs machine sequences.
The handheld robot controller used to jog the robot, change settings, and reset alarms.
A rotating machine axis; in this video it is the axis they test and bleed around.
The up-and-down axis; losing pressure here can let the axis drop.
A power system that uses pressurized fluid to move or hold machine parts.
To let trapped air or fluid out of a hydraulic system through a vent or opened fitting.
A target value the control system uses for pressure or motion decisions.
A hydraulic block that routes fluid to valves and other passages.
A vent point on the hydraulic system where air may be released during bleeding.
An electrically operated valve that opens or closes fluid flow.
A diagram showing electrical connections and components.
A drawing that shows parts separated visually so you can identify them and how they fit together.

domainMetaData.overview: You’re looking at a robot cell where the KUKA teach pendant is used to reset alarms and command motion, while a separate hydraulic system handles part of the machine’s axis or clamp behavior. What’s happening here is that motion depends on both controls and hydraulics: the control side has to be reset correctly, and the hydraulic side has to hold the right pressure so the axis can move and hold safely. The team keeps comparing values from the book, the machine door, and the live settings because not every printed number appears to match what this machine actually needs. Before they open anything, they prove the machine can move again, then they shift into hydraulic diagnosis, looking for vent ports and a manifold so they can bleed trapped air out of the system.

machineIntro.title: KUKA teach pendant and control resets
machineIntro.detail: This is the handheld robot controller you use to navigate the control screen, reset alarms, and command motion. In the video, they distinguish between a PLC alarm reset and a non-PLC alarm reset, which matters because using the right reset is part of getting the machine responsive again.

machineIntro.title: Hydraulic system for the B-axis/clamp
machineIntro.detail: This hydraulic system uses pressure to let the machine move and hold position. You’ll notice they focus on the B-axis first because it can rotate, and they treat that differently from the Z-axis, which could drop if pressure is lost.

machineIntro.title: Set points and pressure references
machineIntro.detail: Set points are the target values the machine uses to decide when pressure is high enough or low enough. In this video, the team compares values in the book, values on the machine door, and live machine settings because they suspect some settings were changed and because the printed diagram may be generic rather than exact for this machine.

machineIntro.title: Manifold and vent ports
machineIntro.detail: The manifold is the hydraulic block where valves and passages come together, and the vent ports are the places they expect to bleed air from the system. Once the cover comes off, that’s the area they’re trying to locate from the diagram.

domainMetaData.overview: You’re looking at a robot cell where the KUKA teach pendant is used to reset alarms and command motion, while a separate hydraulic system handles part of the machine’s axis or clamp behavior. What’s happening here is that motion depends on both controls and hydraulics: the control side has to be reset correctly, and the hydraulic side has to hold the right pressure so the axis can move and hold safely. The team keeps comparing values from the book, the machine door, and the live settings because not every printed number appears to match what this machine actually needs. Before they open anything, they prove the machine can move again, then they shift into hydraulic diagnosis, looking for vent ports and a manifold so they can bleed trapped air out of the system.

machineIntro.title: KUKA teach pendant and control resets
machineIntro.detail: This is the handheld robot controller you use to navigate the control screen, reset alarms, and command motion. In the video, they distinguish between a PLC alarm reset and a non-PLC alarm reset, which matters because using the right reset is part of getting the machine responsive again.

machineIntro.title: Hydraulic system for the B-axis/clamp
machineIntro.detail: This hydraulic system uses pressure to let the machine move and hold position. You’ll notice they focus on the B-axis first because it can rotate, and they treat that differently from the Z-axis, which could drop if pressure is lost.

machineIntro.title: Set points and pressure references
machineIntro.detail: Set points are the target values the machine uses to decide when pressure is high enough or low enough. In this video, the team compares values in the book, values on the machine door, and live machine settings because they suspect some settings were changed and because the printed diagram may be generic rather than exact for this machine.

machineIntro.title: Manifold and vent ports
machineIntro.detail: The manifold is the hydraulic block where valves and passages come together, and the vent ports are the places they expect to bleed air from the system. Once the cover comes off, that’s the area they’re trying to locate from the diagram.
